meta.title: OpenClaw — one local daemon, twenty-two channels
meta.subject: openclaw/openclaw — a personal AI assistant that runs locally and reaches you on every channel you already use

style.rationale: Engineering preset — OpenClaw is a local daemon architecture; argument is about channel topology, queueing semantics, recovery paths.

scenes.title: OpenClaw
scenes.tagline: One brain, every channel you already use.
scenes.footnote: openclaw/openclaw  ·  22 messaging integrations  ·  TypeScript 91.9%  ·  latest release 2026.5.22 — the day before this survey ran

beats.narration: OpenClaw. A self-hosted personal A-I assistant whose local Gateway daemon fans one agent out across twenty-two-plus messaging platforms -- WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, Signal, iMessage, and the rest -- so the assistant reaches you wherever you already talk.

The wager, in one sentence. A single agent runs on your own device, talks back through twenty-two-plus messaging platforms at once, and the rest of the film asks what that aggregation buys you and what it quietly puts on your machine.
We are not here to admire it. We are here to interrogate the position OpenClaw takes -- local brain, many mouths, one daemon -- and to say out loud what its defaults concede.

scenes.heading: Where OpenClaw stands in the field

dimensions.label: Where the brain runs — local vs cloud
Trust model — per-tool prompt vs identity-level vs no host

cells.note: local — agent loop runs in your shell
one terminal — traded breadth for granularity
per-tool prompt — traded flow for per-call consent
hosted backend — the brain lives on OpenAI's servers
one app — traded breadth for zero install
no host access — nothing to reach from a phone
local — runs in your I-D-E process
editor only — traded breadth for code context
rooms and ACLs, not paired senders

novelty.statement: OpenClaw runs a local agent that fans out across 22+ messaging surfaces from one daemon. Claude Code and Cursor stay local but single-surface; Errbot is multi-surface but not L-L-M-native; ChatGPT is multi-app but cloud-hosted.

beats.narration: Four systems share territory with OpenClaw. Claude Code, the Anthropic C-L-I -- local brain, one terminal, per-tool prompts. ChatGPT plus custom G-P-Ts -- a hosted assistant, one app, no host to reach. Cursor and Continue, editor-bound -- local but a single I-D-E. And Errbot and the Mattermost A-I plugins, self-hosted chat bots -- multi-channel, but pre-L-L-M.

Four rows of trade-off the field has made differently. Where the brain runs -- on your machine, or somewhere else. The channel surface -- one place, or every chat in your life. The trust model -- a per-tool prompt, an identity-level pairing, or no host at all. And the shape of the agent -- L-L-M-native, or a scripted bot retrofitted with a model.

Read the brain-location row, and the channel-surface row that pays it off. OpenClaw runs the brain on your laptop, like Claude Code and Cursor; OpenClaw fans the mouth across twenty-two messaging surfaces, like Errbot. Nothing else lives on that intersection -- local-brain, many-channel, L-L-M-native, in one daemon. That is the line OpenClaw draws where its neighbours do not, and the rest of the film is what that line costs.

nodes.label: You
nodes.sub: the only intended user — single operator

nodes.sub: WhatsApp · Telegram · Slack · Discord · Signal · iMessage · IRC · Teams · Matrix · Feishu · LINE · …

nodes.label: Multi-agent routing
nodes.sub: isolated agents per channel/account/peer — workspaces

nodes.label: Gateway daemon
nodes.sub: the only control plane — sessions, channels, tools, events

nodes.label: Companion apps
nodes.sub: macOS menu bar · iOS / Android nodes · Voice Wake

nodes.label: Host (main session)
nodes.sub: tools run with full host access — the README's stated default

nodes.label: Sandbox (opt-in, non-main)
nodes.sub: Docker / S-S-H / OpenShell — only when sandbox.mode = non-main

beats.narration: The Gateway is the architecture, more than any other piece. One local daemon -- launchd on macOS, systemd on Linux -- and it is the only control plane. Sessions, channels, tools, events: all of it lives here. There is no second process, no companion service, no cloud orchestrator.

Around it, the channel adapters. Twenty-two messaging surfaces -- the full list spelled out in the README. Every inbound DM, from every paired channel, lands at this same daemon process. Every outbound reply leaves through the same fan-out. Why the Gateway and not the adapters? Because every adapter terminates here. Lose this, and you lose all of them at once.

Multi-agent routing splits inbound traffic into isolated agent workspaces -- a Discord D-M and an iMessage thread can run in separate sessions, with separate state. The optional companion apps -- macOS menu bar, iOS and Android nodes -- pair to the daemon over its WebSocket; they are the surfaces that make Voice Wake feel always-on.

And the load-bearing default, stated by the README itself. The main session runs on the host with full access -- because, quote, the agent has full access when it is just you. The sandbox modes -- Docker, S-S-H, OpenShell -- exist, but only kick in for non-main sessions, and only if you set the mode explicitly. The main path is unprotected by design.

scenes.heading: How a DM becomes a tool call — or doesn't

actors.label: Main session
actors.sub: full host access — no per-tool prompt

beats.narration: Watch how a single inbound DM becomes -- or fails to become -- a tool call on your laptop. The README is explicit: treat inbound D-Ms as untrusted input. So when an unknown sender messages you on Discord or WhatsApp, the message reaches the Gateway, not the agent.

The Gateway drops the message. It does not forward it. It replies with a pairing code -- the introduction protocol O-penClaw chose -- and waits. That is the architecture failing closed at the door, which is the right place to fail closed.

Now the same flow with a paired sender. Your own phone, allowlisted at install. The Gateway sees the sender on its local allowlist and forwards the message unprompted -- no challenge, no second factor, no per-message consent.

Straight into the main session, which on the default config runs on the host with full reach. And here is the deliberate choice that defines the inner loop: there are no per-tool permission prompts. Claude Code's model -- approve each tool call -- was rejected in favour of identity-level pairing because the always-on, voice-driven feel depends on never stopping to ask. Trust is a property of the sender, not the tool.

The cost of that choice is the next scene. Pairing handles the introduction beautifully. It says nothing about what happens after introduction -- when a paired sender's account, on any one of twenty-two platforms, is itself compromised.

message.label: tool call · no prompt · runs on host

nodes.sub: handles · Mac-bound

nodes.label: ...and seventeen more
nodes.sub: Discord · IRC · Teams · Matrix · Feishu · LINE · Mattermost · Nextcloud Talk · Nostr · Synology Chat · Tlon · Twitch · Zalo · WeChat · QQ · WebChat

nodes.label: Gateway daemon
nodes.sub: one process · ~/.openclaw/workspace · holds every paired credential

nodes.label: Blast radius if reached
nodes.sub: vulnerable extension · leaked workstation · malicious skill in ClawHub → keys to 22 platforms

nodes.label: Recovery cost
nodes.sub: rotate tokens on 22 platforms — most do not make this easy

beats.narration: Now the credential math, made visible. The first time you pair WhatsApp, the daemon stores an O-Auth token. Telegram, a bot token. Slack, a workspace O-Auth grant. Signal, device-linked session keys. iMessage, your Mac-bound handles.

And then the other seventeen. Discord, I-R-C, Microsoft Teams, Matrix, Feishu, LINE, Mattermost, Nextcloud Talk, Nostr, Synology Chat, Tlon, Twitch, Zalo, WeChat, Q-Q, WebChat. Months in, a single host process holds the keys to your entire chat life -- one daemon, twenty-two credential stores.

The geometry is what matters. The blast radius of one local compromise -- a vulnerable extension, a leaked workstation, a malicious skill installed from ClawHub -- is now every paired platform at once. The attacker does not need to pivot. They are already inside every adapter the daemon owns.

And recovery has its own cost. The remediation is rotating tokens on twenty-two platforms, most of which do not make rotation easy -- some require a re-pair on a new device, some invalidate sessions everywhere, some have no programmatic rotation at all. That is the bill the architecture put on your machine, in exchange for being the place you already talk.

nodes.label: Paired sender goes hostile
nodes.sub: an allowlisted account compromised on any of 22 platforms reaches the main session — pairing defends introduction, not ongoing trust

nodes.label: Host execution by default
nodes.sub: main session runs with full host access — no second-line defense when 'just you' stops being true

nodes.label: Voice Wake on a provider hiccup
nodes.sub: 5xx mid-utterance turns 'personal assistant' into 'broken microphone' in perceivable seconds

nodes.label: What was rejected for flow
nodes.sub: per-tool prompts (Claude Code's model) · sandbox-by-default · a hosted backend — each gave up something else

nodes.label: What to watch post-install
nodes.sub: outbound traffic from the Gateway · ~/.openclaw/workspace · `openclaw doctor` after every channel addition

beats.narration: Three failure modes the design genuinely buys, named honestly. The first is the paired-sender hole. Pairing handles the introduction. Once a sender is on your allowlist, they have full reach into the main session. The architecture has nothing left to say when that sender's underlying account -- on any one of those twenty-two platforms -- is itself compromised. The DM walks through the door the system unlocked on day one, with an embedded prompt-injection payload that reaches the agent and, on the host-default config, reaches the disk. There is no in-flight recovery: the credential has already left the host.

The second is the load-bearing default itself. The main session runs on the host with full access -- the README states it; the README also tells you when to override it. But the override is opt-in, on the non-main sessions only. The main path -- the one you, the operator, actually use -- has no second line of defense if a tool call goes wrong. The trade-off was made for flow; the bill arrives the day flow betrays you.

And the most likely three-a-m page. The Voice Wake and Talk Mode loop on macOS and iOS relies on a cloud model plus ElevenLabs T-T-S, with system T-T-S as fallback. When the model provider returns a five-hundred mid-utterance, the assistant degrades from personal-assistant to broken-microphone in perceivable seconds. Failover exists -- the README names auth-profile rotation and fallbacks -- but failover takes time you can hear.

And the alternatives the README's defaults imply were considered and rejected. Per-tool permission prompts -- Claude Code's model -- would have killed the always-on inner loop, so pairing won. Sandbox-by-default would have meant zero friction at the cost of zero second-line defense, so the operator-as-single-user assumption won. A hosted backend -- the ChatGPT shape -- would have lifted credentials off your machine but surrendered the brain, so local won. Each refusal bought flow; each refusal owes the bill the strain row collects.

And the durable thing to watch. Outbound traffic from the Gateway -- the daemon should be talking to your platforms, not to anyone else. The contents of `~/.openclaw/workspace`, where skills live, and a skill is code that runs. And the output of `openclaw doctor` after every channel addition -- the D-M policy drift it surfaces is the regression most likely to bite. That is the on-call you signed up for the moment you ran the install.

Every number in this section is the arithmetic the verdict has to sit on. Twenty-two messaging integrations in one daemon -- the README spells the full list, and that count is what makes the channel-surface row pay off and what gives the credential blast radius its scale.

Three hundred and seventy-four thousand stars on the project. Seventy-seven thousand, eight hundred forks. This is a heavily-watched project, which carries the verdict's risk side: a project at this scale is a target, and star count alone does not buy audit depth.

Fifty-one thousand, eight hundred and fifty-eight commits across one hundred and seventy-four releases. The build manifest matches the framing -- ninety-one-point-nine percent TypeScript. Almost ninety-two percent of the code is the Gateway and adapters; the Swift and Kotlin slices are the optional companion apps. The Gateway really is where the weight lives.

And the number the verdict can't ignore. The latest release shipped one day before this survey ran. A project at this velocity -- one hundred and seventy-four cuts deep, latest landing yesterday -- is not a project that gives reviewers, or operators, time to settle. High-velocity, heavily-watched, single-operator. That is the verdict's posture, expressed as arithmetic, not as opinion.

scenes.heading: OpenClaw — approve with caveats, knowing what you signed up for
points: Disposition — approve with caveats. The architecture is coherent and the README hides nothing about its defaults; that honesty is itself an architectural property.
Strength — the local-brain bet is coherent: one local daemon for twenty-two messaging surfaces is genuinely new, and no other prior system occupies that intersection.
Strength — the README is honest about its defaults rather than hiding them. Host-execution for the main session, pairing-only trust, sandbox opt-in — all stated, all named.
Strength — `openclaw doctor` surfaces D-M policy drift the operator would otherwise miss. The architecture shipped its own canary.
Fragility — paired-sender account compromise on any one of 22 platforms reaches the main session, which inherits full host access. The single biggest residual risk in this review.
Fragility — host-execution by default concedes the second line of defense; the sandbox modes exist but the default does not turn them.
Fragility — the security-default tests cannot be cited from the public surface; the project asks you to trust by reputation, not by named evidence.

When not to choose this — anyone unwilling to be their own on-call for twenty-two channels' worth of credentials sitting on one host. OpenClaw is built for a single operator who will read `openclaw doctor` output and rotate tokens themselves.

beats.narration: The verdict. Approve with caveats. The architecture is coherent, and what is most refreshing about the README is that it hides nothing. The host-execution default, the pairing-only trust model, the sandbox opt-in -- all stated, all named. That honesty is itself an architectural property, and it is what makes this review possible.

Three strengths, named with their evidence. First: the local-brain bet is coherent. One local daemon for twenty-two messaging surfaces is genuinely original -- the prior-art table makes the case. Claude Code and Cursor are local and L-L-M-native, but single-surface; Errbot is multi-surface, but not L-L-M-native; ChatGPT is multi-app, but the brain is in the cloud. OpenClaw sits at the intersection no one else does.

Second: the README is honest about its defaults instead of hiding them -- which is what makes those defaults reviewable at all. And third: the architecture ships its own diagnostic. `openclaw doctor` surfaces D-M policy drift after every channel addition, the regression most likely to bite an unattentive operator, and the project shipping that canary is itself an architectural property worth crediting.

Three fragilities, named just as honestly. First, the single biggest residual risk. A paired sender's account, compromised on any one of twenty-two platforms, reaches the main session. Pairing defends the introduction; it does not defend ongoing trust. The main session inherits full host access, so the failure mode is credential exfiltration in seconds, not minutes.

Second: host execution by default concedes the second line of defense. The sandbox modes are there -- Docker, S-S-H, OpenShell -- but the main path does not turn them. Third: the security-default tests cannot be cited from the public surface. A project at three hundred and seventy-four thousand stars asking the reviewer to trust by reputation, not by named evidence, is itself a finding worth recording.

And the bright line on when not to choose this. Anyone unwilling to be their own on-call for twenty-two channels' worth of credentials sitting on one host. A multi-user setup -- the design assumes one operator. A host you cannot afford to be compromised on, because the main session can reach all of it. Or an operator who will not read `openclaw doctor` output after every channel addition. OpenClaw is a tool for the single operator who is willing to be their own on-call. Knowing that is the verdict; treating the install as anything less is the failure mode.
